4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

It is well-known by now that many software engineers are deeply disturbed by the ongoing advances of AI-assisted programming. While speculation about causes abound — like sadness over not touching programs directly anymore, loss of autonomy, the understandable fear of mistakes, or even just plain laziness — none of them capture the deep spiritual turmoil I've been witnessing. So I was missing a more satisfying explanation, until last month. During vacations, I read again about the pioneering work of Émile Durkheim, considered one of the founders of modern sociology in the XIXth century, partly due to his research on suicide — or, more precisely, his discovery of anomic suicide. It then struck me: anomie is the best diagnosis so far for what many computing professionals are going through.
Anomie, in this context, is defined as a condition whereby the social norms hitherto accepted no longer apply, but no new norms have fully taken their place. In such a situation, individuals become "norm-less" — that is to say, anomic. Durkheim's study of suicide focused on how large social changes, such as the Industrial Revolution, produced this effect and in itself increased suicide rates, regardless of individual characteristics. It is important for sociology because it showed that the social context, in that case the fundamental transformation of economic work relations, could impact even behaviors thought to be very intimate. Now of course there's endless discussion about how to really isolate "social facts" from "individual facts", as Durkheim would postulate, but the anomic relation of individual with respect to their environments is in itself conceptually useful to us.
Anomie is "characterized by a sense of futility, lack of purpose, and emotional emptiness and despair. Striving is considered useless, because there is no accepted definition of what is desirable." [1]
Does that feel familiar? The old way of creating software is gone, we all know it to some extent. But it is not just that developers disagree with the new best practices. Rather, they know that any current best practice is brittle and likely temporary, given the novelty and pace of changes. They find it hard to accept that their purpose is, in reality, largely undefined now. That, circularly, their job now really is to figure out what their job is, if any at all. One gets anxious just by reading this, no?
Later work, notably by Robert K. Merton, highlighted key coping behaviors that we can directly relate to practitioners today:

• Conformity — the individual accepts the new conditions and try to integrate. These are the people honestly embracing the new technology, happily or not. A subset would be better called the enthusiasts, the ones leading the way.

• Ritualism — the individual practices the outward forms that are required, but without true understanding or substance. In technology, we can see at least two subgroups: those using GitHub Copilot or Claude Code just for the sake of consuming tokens and satisfying metrics that their leadership requires, which we can call the bureaucrats; and those who simply enjoy technical novelty for its own sake, regardless of any deeper motivation, which we might call the technicians.

• Retreatism — the individual refuses to remain in the social group. This includes people simply programming as usual, even at the cost of their job prospects, who we can call the obsolete. Soon, if not already, it might mean people who changed professions entirely, the retired.

• Rebellion — the individual rejects the new conditions and, instead, try to impose alternative or older rules. They don't merely retreat; they fight on. These are, for example, the people constantly looking for flaws in AI-assisted programming, not to improve it, but rather to undermine its legitimacy. Let's call this subgroup the saboteurs. Rebels might also have a positive side though: they might reject current practices because they believe in even better AI-enabled methods, and are fighting to prove their point. Let's call these the inventors.

• Innovation — contrary to what the word might suggest, what is invented here is a way to exploit the system maliciously. These individuals accept the value system proposed by the new situation (e.g., make money with AI), but not the way to achieve them. So these are people who do not believe in AI-assisted programming, but pretend to, even to the point of charlatanism and fraud. They want to benefit from the hype, but not truly embrace it in an authentic way. Call them deceivers. We see this also in companies that try to position themselves as AI-first as a mere marketing move, instead of an authentic organization transformation, which ironically gives a troubling new meaning to "product innovation".
There are surely other pathological variations that we can fit in Merton's framework. In practice, this means you should expect to find all of these types in your organization and be prepared to deal with them. You see, it is not just, say, that people are lazy or miss programming all minute details by hand. Even if you somehow force people to adapt and want to turn them into enthusiasts, unless you account for the root causes, you might get bureaucrats instead, or worse. The damage is way deeper — and "innovative" companies only make the problem worse. Conversely, enthusiasts and inventors can be real assets given proper organizational accommodation, such as having space and incentives to experiment, thereby harnessing their creative energy.
As with all such fundamental changes, eventually society will reach a new point of normative equilibrium and generally accepted practices. The point is what to do in the short and medium terms, before this happens. Awareness of the social and psychological forces shaping the landscape is a critical factor in planning that properly.
